name: Gaya
classification_notes: Gaya follows text character structure with organic, rounded forms derived from the study of blur effects on typography, placing it firmly in the humanist sans tradition with an expressive, fluid character.
summary: A humanist sans with soft, rounded forms derived from studying the blurring of type, designed for cultural institutions and editorial work.
description: Designed by Raphaël de La Morinerie and available since 2020, Gaya draws its character from the organic effects of blur on letterforms, producing curved shapes that harmonise across weights from Thin to Black. The italic cuts amplify the fluid quality, reinforced by an extensive ligature set. The family has found use in concert hall communications, festivals, book layouts and magazine design.
foundry_blurb: Writtenshape describe Gaya as drawing inspiration from objects shaped by ordinary use, whose forms reflect longevity and efficiency. The design originates from a study of blur effects on typography, resulting in curved, rounded letterforms and a fluid expression that is further emphasised in the italics and a large number of ligatures. Gaya sits between independent artistic projects and cultural institution identities, and has been used in concert halls, festivals, books and magazines.

name: Reptil
designer: Raphaël de La Morinerie & Ethan Nakache
classification_notes: Reptil is a modular display typeface built entirely around circular forms, reviving the geometric construction logic of Letraset's Turtle (1971) with a contemporary precision. Its compacted capitals and strikingly shaped lowercase push legibility to its limits, placing it firmly in the display category.
summary: A circular-modular revival of Bob Newman's 1971 Letraset typeface Turtle, rebuilt from first principles by two French designers.
description: Built on the discovery that the original Turtle was constructed entirely from circular shapes, Raphaël de La Morinerie and Ethan Nakache extended that modular logic into a complete, contemporary character set. Compact capitals form dense typographic blocks, while the lowercase retains the eccentric legibility-limit quality of the source material. Available in a single Regular weight with broad Latin Extended coverage, it suits editorial, poster and identity work where geometric retro energy is the point.
foundry_blurb: Writtenshape describe Reptil as a contemporary interpretation of Turtle, a 1971 Letraset typeface by British designer Bob Newman, known for Data Seventy and Frankfurter. After studying the original, the designers identified its circular construction logic and used that as the basis for a complete, precision-built character set. They position it as a vintage typeface with a contemporary touch, one that tests the limits of legibility while remaining clearly visible, with compact capitals that structure layouts and a striking lowercase that surprises the eye.

name: Naive
designer: Raphaël de La Morinerie & Benoît Brun
classification_notes: Circle-based letterforms with psychedelic and geometric construction, derived from Flemming Bau's 1970s Bau typeface; clearly display-oriented with shapes that resist instant reading and suit headline and poster use.
summary: A single-weight geometric display face reinterpreting Flemming Bau's circle-based 1972 typeface, extended with new uppercase characters for contemporary use.
description: Built on the circle-based logic of Flemming Bau's original 1972 Bau typeface, this revival adds a full uppercase set drawn on a geometric system that was absent from the source material. The letterforms carry a deliberately resistant quality, slowing reading in favour of visual impact, which makes them suited to posters, logotypes and headlines where attention is the primary goal. Designed by Raphaël de La Morinerie and Benoît Brun at Writtenshape, it covers an extended Latin character set.
foundry_blurb: Writtenshape describe Naive as a free interpretation of Bau, a font designed by Flemming Bau that won a Mecanorma typography competition in 1972. Bau's circle-based letters combined psychedelic flowing elements with strict geometric construction, and Writtenshape have redrawn and extended this with new uppercase characters. The result is positioned for edgy headlines, logotypes and psychedelic posters where shapes are intended to catch the eye and surprise the reader.

name: Craft
designer: Benoît Brun & Raphaël de La Morinerie
classification_notes: Craft is a display typeface inspired by ancient wooden titling characters, with a monocase construction where all glyphs are contained within the x-height and connect to one another, placing it firmly in the display category with a rustic, experimental character.
summary: A monocase display typeface inspired by ancient wooden titling characters, where all glyphs sit within the x-height and link together to form continuous words.
description: Designed by Benoît Brun and Raphaël de La Morinerie, it draws on the heritage of wood type while departing from conventional letterform construction: every glyph is contained within the x-height and connects seamlessly to its neighbours. Accents extend above the x-height and below the baseline to preserve readability without disrupting the monocase logic. Available in two styles, upright and italic, with OpenType options to customise the connecting behaviour.
foundry_blurb: Writtenshape describe Craft as a bold, alternative typeface rooted in the history of titling typography and ancient wooden characters. Its rustic charm is the starting point, but the design pushes against conventional typographic norms by confining all glyphs to the x-height and joining them together, creating words and phrases built for visual impact and daring brand identities.

name: Kateka
classification_notes: Kateka draws on baroque details from 18th-century Dutch typographer Joan Michaël Fleischmann, placing it firmly in the transitional serif tradition, with a rationalist digital approach that modernises the historical forms.
summary: A text serif rooted in the baroque letterforms of Joan Michaël Fleischmann, rationalised through a straight-line digital drawing method for screen legibility.
description: Drawn by Raphaël de La Morinerie, Kateka takes cues from 18th-century Dutch punch-cutter Joan Michaël Fleischmann and rebuilds those baroque proportions using straight lines wherever possible, reducing curve complexity to sharpen rendering on screen. The result is a high-contrast text face with a pronounced slant in the italic, lending it enough presence for headings while keeping body text comfortable. Three styles are available: Regular, Bold and Italic.
foundry_blurb: Writtenshape describe Kateka as an original creation informed by in-depth research into text typography, drawing on baroque details from the characters of 18th-century Dutch typographer Joan Michaël Fleischmann. Traditional text forms were rationalised through a digital approach in which each glyph is drawn with as many straight lines as possible, optimising screen readability while giving the face a distinctly modern character. Its high contrast and the pronounced slant of the italic allow it to stand out in headings, while clean shapes ensure reading comfort in body text.

name: Adapt
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a neo-grotesque inspired by Swiss modernist typography, with tubular angles on certain glyphs referencing 19th-century American sans-serif typefaces.
summary: A Swiss-modernist neo-grotesque with calm proportions and subtle nods to 19th-century American sans-serifs, suited to long-form text and signage alike.
description: Designed by Raphaël de La Morinerie, Adapt draws on Swiss modernist tradition while introducing tubular angled terminals on glyphs such as J, f and t that recall early American sans-serifs. Ample capital proportions and increased contrast in the bolder weights give the family a measured vintage character without undermining its legibility at text sizes. Seven weights from Thin to Black cover everyday editorial and wayfinding work, and a PDF specimen details the layout-oriented OpenType features.
foundry_blurb: Writtenshape describe Adapt as a neo-grotesque defined by calm and carefully crafted shapes, inspired by Swiss modernist typography and designed for everyday use and precise text composition. At larger scales the tubular angles of glyphs like J, f and t evoke 19th-century American sans-serifs, while the contrast in bolder styles adds vintage character for headlines. They note it is suited to everything from simple invoices to signage on large architectural projects.
